questions.question: What is the clearest reason to choose a stream instead of a queue?
options: Several independent consumers need durable replayable history from the same producer output.
A single worker needs to finish one background task after another.
The system wants to avoid thinking about ordering and offsets.
The producer does not want to persist events anywhere.
questions.explanation: Streams are strongest when multiple consumers need shared durable history and replay.

questions.question: What usually makes a workflow engine necessary?
options: The system must track long-running state, timers, retries, and step-by-step progress explicitly.
The system only needs a fast single-step image resize task.
The team wants fewer state transitions in the business process.
The producer never needs to know whether work completed.
questions.explanation: Workflow engines are for stateful orchestration, not just asynchronous dispatch.

questions.question: Which design is weakest?
options: Use a queue for one-owner execution and publish events separately for analytics replay.
Use a workflow engine when multi-step business progress and timers must be visible.
Use a stream because multiple consumers need independent replay and offset control.
Use Kafka for all async needs without clarifying consumer ownership, replay, or business state.
questions.explanation: Naming a product without stating the required semantics is the anti-pattern.

questions.question: What hidden risk appears when a team uses only a queue for an event-sharing problem?
options: New downstream consumers often need ad hoc taps or copied databases because replayable shared history is missing.
The queue automatically creates too many workflow timers.
Consumers will always get strict global ordering for free.
The producer no longer needs idempotency.
questions.explanation: Queues are poor at durable one-to-many replay unless extra systems are added.

questions.question: Which metric most directly distinguishes task execution problems from history distribution problems?
options: The number of independent consumer groups and their replay needs.
Only CPU usage on one producer host.
The number of developers on the team.
Average JSON document size in the codebase.
questions.explanation: Consumer independence and replay expectations are core primitive-selection inputs.

questions.question: Why is 'exactly-once' still not enough to skip idempotent handlers?
options: Because side effects outside the broker boundary can still be repeated or partially applied.
Because brokers do not preserve partition ordering.
Because queues cannot retry failed tasks.
Because workflow engines remove the need for durable storage.
questions.explanation: Broker guarantees do not fully cover downstream side effects.

questions.question: Which observability signal best shows an async architecture is mismatched to the product?
options: Teams keep building bespoke replay tools and shadow status tables around the primitive.
The producer logs include request IDs.
Consumers expose CPU metrics.
The docs mention eventual consistency.
questions.explanation: Repeated compensating systems often reveal the primitive itself is the wrong fit.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with choosing a workflow engine over a plain queue?
options: You gain explicit state, timers, and compensation, but you pay with orchestration metadata and platform complexity.
You gain global exactly-once side effects with no extra cost.
You remove the need for retries and timeouts entirely.
You make long-running jobs cheaper than stateless fire-and-forget tasks.
questions.explanation: Workflow engines buy stateful orchestration at the cost of more system complexity.
